**Labuan** ([/ləˈbuːən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), officially the **Federal Territory of Labuan** ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Wilayah Persekutuan Labuan*), is an island [federal territory](/wiki/Federal_Territories_of_Malaysia "Federal Territories of Malaysia") of Malaysia. It includes *Labuan Island* and six smaller islands off the coast of the state of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") in East Malaysia. Labuan's capital is [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Labuan "Victoria, Labuan"), which is best known as an [offshore financial centre](/wiki/Offshore_financial_centre "Offshore financial centre") offering international financial and business services via [Labuan IBFC](/wiki/Labuan_IBFC "Labuan IBFC") since 1990, as well as being an offshore support hub for deepwater oil and gas activities in the region. It is also a tourist destination for people travelling through Sabah, nearby [Bruneians](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), and [scuba divers](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving"). The name Labuan derives from the [Malay word](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") *labuhan*, which means "harbour".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

For three centuries from the 15th century, the north and west coast of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo") including the island of Labuan was part of the [Sultanate of Brunei](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire").[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[7]](#cite_note-McColl2005-7) In 1775, Labuan was temporarily occupied by the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") after the failure of the company's station at [Balambangan Island](/wiki/Balambangan_Island "Balambangan Island"). The Sultan of Brunei, [Omar Ali Saifuddin I](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_I "Omar Ali Saifuddin I") tried to secure the British East India Company's support against the [Sulu](/wiki/Sulu "Sulu") raiders by offering Labuan as an alternative to Balambangan but, instead the company made a second but failed attempt to set up a station on Balambangan. The Sultan's subsequent reoffering of Labuan to the company did not result in the establishment of a permanent British settlement on the island either.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

British policy changed in the 19th century, when Labuan started to attract British interest again. [Captain Rodney Mundy](/wiki/Rodney_Mundy "Rodney Mundy") acquired the island for Britain through the [Treaty of Labuan](/wiki/Treaty_of_Labuan "Treaty of Labuan") with the Sultan of Brunei, [Omar Ali Saifuddin II](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_II "Omar Ali Saifuddin II") on 18 December 1846.[[16]](#cite_note-Murray-16) Mundy took [Pengiran Mumin](/wiki/Abdul_Momin "Abdul Momin") to witness the island's accession to the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") on 24 December 1846.[[17]](#cite_note-Saunders2013-17) Some sources state that during the signing of the treaty, the Sultan had been threatened by a [British navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") warship ready to fire on the Sultan's palace if he refused to sign the treaty while another source says the island was ceded to Britain as a reward for assistance in combating pirates.[[8]](#cite_note-lb-8)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The main reason why the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") acquired the island was to establish a naval station to protect their commercial interest in the region and to suppress piracy in the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea").[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The British also believed the island could be the next [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_in_the_Straits_Settlements "Singapore in the Straits Settlements").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Following the [Anglo-Brunei Treaty of Friendship](/wiki/Anglo-Brunei_Treaty_of_Friendship_1847 "Anglo-Brunei Treaty of Friendship 1847") in 1847, which distilled the aforementioned events into a single document - Labuan was ceded to the British in perpetuity.[[23]](#cite_note-:wright-23) The island became a [Crown Colony](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Labuan "Crown Colony of Labuan") in 1848 with [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke") appointed as the first governor and [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief"), with [William Napier](/wiki/William_Napier_(lawyer) "William Napier (lawyer)") as his lieutenant-governor.[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[9]](#cite_note-lbc-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Mathews2014-10)[[16]](#cite_note-Murray-16) In 1849, the [Eastern Archipelago Company](/wiki/Eastern_Archipelago_Company "Eastern Archipelago Company") became the first of several British companies to try to exploit Labuan coal deposits.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The company was formed to exploit coal deposits on the island and adjacent coast of Borneo but soon became involved in a dispute with [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke").[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Not proving itself a great commercial or strategic asset, administration of Labuan was handed to the [British North Borneo Company](/wiki/British_North_Borneo_Company "British North Borneo Company") in 1890.[[27]](#cite_note-OlsonShadle1996-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Welman-28) The North Borneo Company did not prove that effective a manager either, and in 1904 [Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur](/wiki/Malcolm_Stewart_Hannibal_McArthur "Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur") even proposed handing the island back to Brunei.[[29]](#cite_note-deVienne2015-29) In 1894, a [submarine communications cable](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable") was built by the British to link the island's communications with [North Borneo](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo"), Singapore and [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") for the first time.[[30]](#cite_note-30) By 30 October 1906, the British Government proposed to extend the boundaries of the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements") to include Labuan. The proposal took effect from 1 January 1907.[[11]](#cite_note-RentonRobertson1908-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Office1917-12)

[Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy") anchoring at the coast of Labuan on 14 January 1942

In [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Labuan was [occupied](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_British_Borneo "Japanese occupation of British Borneo") by [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") from 3 January 1942 until June 1945 and garrisoned by units of the [Japanese 37th Army](/wiki/Thirty-Seventh_Army_(Japan) "Thirty-Seventh Army (Japan)"), which controlled Northern Borneo.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The island served as the administrative centre for the Japanese forces.[[35]](#cite_note-Ooi2004-35) During the occupation, the Japanese Government changed the island name to **Maida Island** (前田島 [*Maeda-shima*]) on 9 December 1942 after [Marquis](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess") [Toshinari Maeda](/wiki/Toshinari_Maeda "Toshinari Maeda"), as a remembrance to the first Japanese commander in northern Borneo. Maeda was killed in an [air crash](/wiki/Aviation_accidents_and_incidents "Aviation accidents and incidents") at [Bintulu](/wiki/Bintulu "Bintulu"), Sarawak when en route to the island to open the airfield there.[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[35]](#cite_note-Ooi2004-35) As the Allied counter-attack came closer, the Japanese also developed Labuan and [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay") as a naval base.[[17]](#cite_note-Saunders2013-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/American_Support_Craft_%28AWM_108818%29.jpg/250px-American_Support_Craft_%28AWM_108818%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:American_Support_Craft_(AWM_108818).jpg)

American [support craft](/wiki/Landing_craft "Landing craft") moving towards [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Labuan "Victoria, Labuan") and Brown beach to assist the landing of the members of Australian [24th Infantry Brigade](/wiki/24th_Brigade_(Australia) "24th Brigade (Australia)") on the island during [Operation Oboe Six](/wiki/Operation_Oboe_Six "Operation Oboe Six")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Japanese_Surrender%2C_Labuan_%28AWM_115989%29.JPG/250px-Japanese_Surrender%2C_Labuan_%28AWM_115989%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Japanese_Surrender,_Labuan_(AWM_115989).JPG)

Japanese Commander in Borneo, Lieutenant General [Masao Baba](/wiki/Masao_Baba "Masao Baba") signing the surrender document dated 9 September 1945 on the [Australian 9th Division](/wiki/9th_Division_(Australia) "9th Division (Australia)") headquarters in Labuan while being watched by the [Australian Major General](/wiki/Major_general_(Australia) "Major general (Australia)") [George Wootten](/wiki/George_Wootten "George Wootten")

The liberation of Borneo by the [Allied forces](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") began on 10 June 1945 when the Australian Army under the command of [Australian Major General](/wiki/Major_general_(Australia) "Major general (Australia)") George Wootten launched an attack under the codename of [Operation Oboe Six](/wiki/Battle_of_North_Borneo "Battle of North Borneo"). Labuan became the main objective for the Allied forces to repossess.[[36]](#cite_note-Sandler2001-36) Soon, the [9th Division](/wiki/9th_Division_(Australia) "9th Division (Australia)") of the Australian Army launched the attack with support from airstrikes and sea bombardments until the capture of the Labuan airstrip.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Most of the Labuan island area including the main town of [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Labuan "Victoria, Labuan") was under the control of Allied forces within four days of the [landing on 10 June](/wiki/Battle_of_Labuan "Battle of Labuan").[[36]](#cite_note-Sandler2001-36) On 9 September 1945, the Japanese Lieutenant General [Masao Baba](/wiki/Masao_Baba "Masao Baba") officially surrendered at a place now known as Surrender Point near the Layang-layang beach which he had been brought to the 9th Division headquarters on the island to sign the surrender document in front of the Australian 9th Division Army Commander [George Wootten](/wiki/George_Wootten "George Wootten").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The name of Labuan was later restored by the British and the island was administered under the [British Military Administration](/wiki/British_Military_Administration_(Borneo) "British Military Administration (Borneo)") together with the rest of the Straits Settlements. Labuan then on 15 July 1946 joined the [North Borneo Crown Colony](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_North_Borneo "Crown Colony of North Borneo"), which in turn became the state of Sabah and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") in 1963.[[6]](#cite_note-lb_history-6)[[13]](#cite_note-Kratoska2001-13) In 1984, the Government of Sabah ceded Labuan to the [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia") and later was admitted as a [federal territory](/wiki/Federal_Territories_(Malaysia) "Federal Territories (Malaysia)"). This was done so that it could become an offshore financial centre.[[14]](#cite_note-labuan-14) It was declared an international offshore financial centre and [free trade zone](/wiki/Free_trade_zone "Free trade zone") in 1990.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Federal_Territory_of_Labuan.svg/250px-Federal_Territory_of_Labuan.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_Territory_of_Labuan.svg)

A map of Labuan island including its outlying islands

Labuan's area comprises the main island (Labuan Island – 91.64 square kilometres or 35.38 square miles) and six other smaller islands, Burung, [Daat](/wiki/Daat_Island "Daat Island"), [Kuraman](/wiki/Kuraman_Island "Kuraman Island"), Big Rusukan, Small Rusukan and Papan island with a total area of 91.64 square kilometres (35.38 [square miles](/wiki/Square_mile "Square mile")). The islands lie 8 kilometres (5.0 miles) off the coast of Borneo, adjacent to the [Malaysian state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia") of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and to the north of [Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), on the northern edge of [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay") facing the South China Sea. Labuan Island is mainly flat and undulating; its highest point is [Bukit Kubong](/w/index.php?title=Bukit_Kubong&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bukit Kubong (page does not exist)") at 148 metres (486 feet) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level"). Over 70% of the island is still covered with vegetation. The main town area of Victoria is located in a position facing Brunei Bay.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Labuan has a tropical rainforest climate with no dry season. Over the course of a year, the temperature typically varies from 25 to 32 °C (77 to 90 °F) and is rarely below 24 °C (75 °F) or above 33 °C (91 °F). The warm season lasts from 1 April to 13 June with an average daily high temperature above 31 °C (88 °F). The hottest day of the year is 29 April, with an average high of 32 °C (90 °F) and low of 26 °C (79 °F). The cold season lasts from 7 January to 17 February with an average daily high temperature below 30 °C (86 °F). The coldest day of the year is 8 September, with an average low of 25 °C (77 °F) and high of 31 °C (88 °F).[[41]](#cite_note-weather-41) The weather station for Labuan is located at [Labuan Airport](/wiki/Labuan_Airport "Labuan Airport").

Thunderstorms are the most severe precipitation observed in Labuan during 60% of those days with precipitation. They are most likely around October, when they occur very frequently. Meanwhile, the relative humidity for Labuan typically ranges from 63% (mildly humid) to 96% (very humid) over the course of the year, rarely dropping below 53% and reaching as high as 100% (extremely humid).[[41]](#cite_note-weather-41)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

Labuan is one of the Malaysian federal government territories. The island is administered by the federal government through the Department of Federal Territories, a department under the [Prime Minister's Department (Malaysia)](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Department_(Malaysia) "Prime Minister's Department (Malaysia)"). [Labuan Corporation](/wiki/Labuan_Corporation "Labuan Corporation") is the municipal government for the island and is headed by a chairman who is responsible for the development and administration of the island. Labuan has one representative in each of the Lower and Upper Houses of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia"). Typically, the current member of the parliament of Labuan will be appointed to become chairman of Labuan Corporation.

The island is represented in the lower house of parliament by MP Suhaili Abdul Rahman of PN (opposition) and in the upper house by Mohd Husni bin Mohamad Salleh.

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Security")]

Security is the responsibility of the federal government, with naval patrol vessels, a garrison, and an air detachment based on the island. The vigilance of the local Coast Guard, Customs and Excise and Marine Police contribute to the maintenance of Labuan's reputation and status as an international offshore financial centre and free trade zone.

Kwang Fook Kong Temple

According to Malaysia's Department of Statistics, Labuan's population was 86,908 at the 2010 Census and 95,120 at the 2020 Census.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The ethnic composition in 2020 in Labuan was: [Kedayan](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan") and [Bruneian Malay](/wiki/Bruneian_Malay "Bruneian Malay") (34,068), [Kadazan-Dusun](/wiki/Kadazan-Dusun "Kadazan-Dusun") including/excluding [Rungus](/wiki/Rungus_people "Rungus people") (9,408), [Bajau](/wiki/Bajau_people "Bajau people") (6,866), [Murut](/wiki/Murut_people "Murut people") (1265), [Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") (9,843), [Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indians "Malaysian Indians") (891), [Lundayeh](/wiki/Lundayeh "Lundayeh") and other ethnic groups (21,947), and non-Malaysian citizens (10,832). The majority of Chinese people in Labuan are from the [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") dialect group (but has been since decreasing due to migration to the [Peninsula](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") as well as overseas); however, there are also many [Hakkas](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people"), most of whom are migrants or descendants of migrants from mainland Sabah as well as local-born Hakkas, whom are settled for more than 2 to 3 generations in the island dating as long as the [colonial period](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo"), in which they came as stopover migrants or traders before embarking to the mainland (even when it was still an only island offshore municipality within [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") state jurisdiction from 1963 to 1984) as well as a negligible minority of [Fuzhounese](/wiki/Fuzhou_people "Fuzhou people") from neighbouring Sarawak, mostly working as government staff and those posted in the petroleum and gas offshore industries and to a lesser extent a large minority of [Dayaks](/wiki/Dayak_people "Dayak people") also from Sarawak who are resident here and their local-born descendants whom are mostly ethnically [Iban](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people") with pockets of [Bidayuh](/wiki/Bidayuh "Bidayuh") and [Orang Ulu](/wiki/Orang_Ulu "Orang Ulu") residents.

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit) Census the population of Labuan is 76.0% [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), 12.4% Christian, 9.0% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), 0.4% [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu"), 2.1% follower of other religions, and 0.1% non-religious.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Labuan_port-Malaysia.JPG/250px-Labuan_port-Malaysia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Labuan_port-Malaysia.JPG)

Labuan Port

The economy of Labuan thrives on its vast oil and gas resources and international investment and banking services. Labuan is very much an import-export oriented economy. Virtually all of its commodities including [crude oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol"), HBI,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] [gas](/wiki/Natural_Gas "Natural Gas"), flour, animal feed, sea products, and ceramic tiles are exported either to Peninsular Malaysia or overseas. Raw materials, parts, and equipments for industrial uses well as consumer products are imported. In 2004, the total value of Labuan's external trade reached MYR11.8 billion from only MYR5.0 billion in 1995 for a net trade surplus of MYR5.1 billion. Among its major trade partners are India, Peninsular Malaysia, Sarawak, and South Korea. 65% of its exports are petroleum and gas-based products.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of Labuan is estimated at MYR3.63 billion in 2012 with a growth rate of 5.8 per cent. Labuan GDP per capita in 2012 is MYR39,682. The total employment for Labuan is around 39,800 in 2012. The main economic sectors in Labuan is service and manufacturing which contributed 94.6 per cent to the island GDP. The service sector consisted mainly of Finance and Insurance and Real Estate and Business Services. Meanwhile, the manufacturing sector consists mainly of oil and gas industry and support.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Labuan_Malaysia_Financial-Park-Complex-01.jpg/250px-Labuan_Malaysia_Financial-Park-Complex-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Labuan_Malaysia_Financial-Park-Complex-01.jpg)

Labuan Financial Park complex, also known as Ujana Kewangan (UK) by many Labuanese.

The Labuan International Business and Financial Centre [Labuan IBFC](/wiki/Labuan_IBFC "Labuan IBFC") was created as Malaysia's only offshore financial hub in October 1990 and was operating under the name of Labuan International Offshore Financial Centre (IOFC). At the time it was established to strengthen the contribution of financial services to the [Gross National Product](/wiki/Gross_National_Product "Gross National Product") (GNP) of Malaysia as well as to develop the island and its surrounding vicinity. The jurisdiction, supervised by the Labuan Offshore Financial Services Authority or LOFSA, offers benefits such as 3% tax[[52]](#cite_note-52) on net audited results or a flat rate of Malaysian Ringgit (MYR) 20,000 to trading companies; low operational costs; liberal exchange controls; and a host of other advantages including readily available, experienced and professional service providers. In 2010 the notion "offshore" was excluded from all the statutes of Labuan due to world pressure on the tax havens and offshores.

Since its inception, the jurisdiction has expanded to become a base for more than 4,800 [offshore companies](/wiki/Offshore_company "Offshore company") and 68 licensed banks. The IBFC also has over 370 registered foundations, 100 partnerships, and over 63 licensed trusts.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Labuan IBFC is embarking on an aggressive growth strategy to become the premier international business and financial centre in the Asia Pacific region.

Labuan's business focus is on five core areas: offshore holding companies, captive insurance, Shariah-compliant Islamic Finance structures, public and private funds, and wealth management. Labuan IBFC's position is further enhanced by the launch of the Malaysian International Islamic Finance Centre initiative in August 2006.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Development")]

Unlike other federal territory, Labuan is underdeveloped. The island's urban landscape is characterised by a mix of older buildings and limited modern architectural advancements. Key infrastructure projects, such as road networks and public transportation systems, are less advanced nor extensive, resulting in significant traffic issues during peak hours, particularly in the early morning and evening commutes.

### Labuan-Menumbok Bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Labuan-Menumbok Bridge")]

One of the most anticipated infrastructure projects is the proposed bridge connecting Labuan to Menumbok in mainland Sabah. Like [Penang Bridge](/wiki/Penang_Bridge "Penang Bridge"). Labuan-Menumbok bridge aims to enhance connectivity and stimulate economic growth by providing a direct link between the island and the mainland. The project is expected to reduce travel time, improve the movement of goods and people, and boost tourism in the region, reflecting their commitment to regional development and integration.

The proposal was first introduced in the 1990s. However, it has faced numerous delays due to fact that the feasibility studies for the project were outdated and needed to be updated for the project to proceed smoothly.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Transport")]

Labuan offers two primary modes of transportation for travel to and from the island.

### Water ferry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Water ferry")]

[![White ferry with "Labuan Express 2" painted on the side](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/KotaKinabalu_Sabah_JesseltonPoint-07.jpg/250px-KotaKinabalu_Sabah_JesseltonPoint-07.jpg)](/wiki/File:KotaKinabalu_Sabah_JesseltonPoint-07.jpg)

A ferry from Labuan in [Kota Kinabalu](/wiki/Kota_Kinabalu "Kota Kinabalu"), Sabah

Labuan Ferry: One of the main routes is via water ferry services to Sabah and Sarawak. This ferry service is a vital link for passengers and vehicles, providing a convenient and scenic route across the island's strait.

In May 2024, [Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim "Anwar Ibrahim") emphasised the need to update existing technical studies to reflect the current situation, such as upgrading works at the terminal, which costed RM22 Million. The funding is intended to enhance the facilities, infrastructure, jetty, and ferry ramp at the terminal. The Prime Minister said the estimated one-hour embarkation time could be reduced by addressing delays caused by immigration checks. He emphasised that efficient services from local enforcement agencies were crucial in resolving congestion issues at the terminal, rather than relying solely on new infrastructure.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Ferry services between [Kota Kinabalu](/wiki/Kota_Kinabalu "Kota Kinabalu"), Sabah, and Labuan were suspended after the [COVID-19 pandemic in Malaysia](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Malaysia "COVID-19 pandemic in Malaysia"). Initially scheduled to resume on 15 December 2024 from Labuan to [Jesselton Point](/w/index.php?title=Jesselton_Point&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jesselton Point (page does not exist)"), further delays occurred due to a lack of readiness as Jesselton Point.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The ferry service eventually resumed on 28 May, although connecting to [Kota Kinabalu Port](/w/index.php?title=Kota_Kinabalu_Port&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kota Kinabalu Port (page does not exist)") instead of Jesselton Point.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Air travel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Air travel")]

[Labuan Airport](/wiki/Labuan_Airport "Labuan Airport") facilitates air travel to the major destinations. The airport hosts several airlines:

* **Malaysia Airlines**: Provides regular flights to Kuala Lumpur, the capital city of Malaysia, offering connectivity to both domestic and international destinations.
* **AirAsia**: Another major airline operating from Labuan Airport, AirAsia also offers flights to Kuala Lumpur, catering to budget-conscious travellers.
* **MASwings**: A regional airline, MASwings, operates flights to Miri in Sarawak as well as Kota Kinabalu in the neighbouring state of Sabah, enhancing connectivity within East Malaysia.

These transportation options ensure that W.P. Labuan remains accessible and well-connected, supporting both the local population and visitors to the island.

## Places of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Places of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Labuan-Chimney.jpg/250px-Labuan-Chimney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Labuan-Chimney.jpg)

[Chimney](/wiki/Chimney_Museum "Chimney Museum") at the Colliery Fields

There are several attractions and places of interest in Labuan. The [Labuan War Cemetery](/wiki/Labuan_War_Cemetery "Labuan War Cemetery") contains various war graves and memorials to the fallen of World War II. This includes British, Australian, Indian, Sarawakian, Bruneian, North Bornean, and Empire troops, with a total of 3,908 graves of fallen soldiers. A memorial service is held on Remembrance Day once every four years. The territory also has a memorial celebrating the surrender of the Japanese to the Australian Forces in 1945. Remnants of Labuan's history as a [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") [coaling station](/wiki/Coaling_station "Coaling station") include [the chimney](/wiki/Chimney_Museum "Chimney Museum"), a well known local landmark. Labuan also contains a [Labuan Maritime Museum](/wiki/Labuan_Maritime_Museum "Labuan Maritime Museum").

Labuan is also the base for diving on four popular wreck dives: the Cement wreck, the American wreck (the first [USS *Salute*](/wiki/USS_Salute_(AM-294) "USS Salute (AM-294)")), the Australian wreck and the Blue Water wreck.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Labuan has many schools. However, it has only one international school, Labuan International School.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Other places of interest include the Labuan International Sea Sport Complex. Newly proposed is the Marina Centre and Labuan Square project which were completed in 2010.

Labuan's own institution of higher education is Universiti Malaysia Sabah Labuan International Campus,[[60]](#cite_note-60) a branch of Universiti Malaysia Sabah in Sepanggar Bay, [Kota Kinabalu](/wiki/Kota_Kinabalu "Kota Kinabalu"). Labuan also has [Kolej Matrikulasi Labuan](/wiki/Kolej_Matrikulasi_Labuan "Kolej Matrikulasi Labuan"), the only matriculation college in East Malaysia. All pre-university students from [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"), [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), and Labuan take their courses at this institution.

## Postage stamps and postal history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Labuan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Postage stamps and postal history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Stamp_Labuan_1885_2c.jpg/250px-Stamp_Labuan_1885_2c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamp_Labuan_1885_2c.jpg)

1885 2c stamp

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/2c_stamp_of_labuan_north_borneo.jpg/250px-2c_stamp_of_labuan_north_borneo.jpg)](/wiki/File:2c_stamp_of_labuan_north_borneo.jpg)

Stamp of North Borneo overprinted "Labuan", 1897

A post office was operating in Labuan by 1864, and used a circular date stamp as [postmark](/wiki/Postmark "Postmark"). The postage stamps of [India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") and Hong Kong were used on some mail, but they were probably carried there by individuals, instead of being on sale in Labuan. Mail was routed through Singapore. From 1867, Labuan officially used the postage stamps of the Straits Settlements but began issuing its own in May 1879.

Although initially the design for the first stamp issue was proposed to be depicting a clump of [sago palms](/wiki/Sago_palm "Sago palm"), for economic reasons, the queen heads design was finally adopted, having been used initially for postage stamps of [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada").[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) The first stamps of Labuan therefore depict the usual profile of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom") but are unusual for being inscribed in Malay-Arabic ([Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script")) and Chinese scripts in addition to "LABUAN POSTAGE". Perennial shortages necessitated a variety of [surcharges](/wiki/Fee "Fee") in between the several reprints and colour changes of the 1880s. The original stamps were engraved, but the last of the design, in April 1894, were done by [lithography](/wiki/Lithography "Lithography").

Beginning in May 1894, the stamps of North Borneo were overprinted "LABUAN". On 24 September 1896, the 50th anniversary of the cession was marked by overprinting "1846 / JUBILEE / 1896" on North Borneo stamps. Additional overprints appeared through the 1890s. In 1899 many types were surcharged with a value of 4 cents.

The last Labuan-only design came out in 1902, depicting a crown and inscribed "LABUAN COLONY". After incorporation into the Straits Settlements in 1906, Labuan ceased issuing its own stamps, although they remained valid for some time. Many of the remainder were [cancelled-to-order](/wiki/Cancelled-to-order "Cancelled-to-order") for sale to collectors and are now worth only pennies; genuine franked/post used stamps are worth much more.
